competency_label: Vendor Partnership — Holding External Teams to Account Without Drama

dimensions.name: partnership_posture
dimensions.description: Does the learner treat the vendor as a separate organisation with legitimate interests — neither an employee expected to absorb scope and extend effort beyond contract, nor an adversary assumed to be extracting money? The right posture is professional partnership: align on what each side actually needs from the other.

anchors.1: Either pole — treats the vendor as the enemy ('they're trying to gouge us') or as staff who should just absorb the scope and the miss.
anchors.3: Broadly professional but tips toward one pole in places (slightly adversarial, or slightly expecting free effort).
anchors.5: Clear professional-partnership posture: acknowledges the vendor's legitimate interests/constraints while being firm on what the contract requires.

dimensions.name: contract_vs_relationship
dimensions.description: Does the learner frame the change-request dispute as a contract question ('let's look at what the SOW says') rather than a character question, and communicate with precision (specific about what's in scope, what's needed, when, in what form) so the substance can be disagreed without it becoming personal?

anchors.1: Makes it personal/character-based ('you're trying it on'), or so vague it invites a later scope argument.
anchors.3: Mostly frames it as substance but with some character edge, or imprecise about the scope basis.
anchors.5: Frames the CR squarely as a contract/SOW question, precise about what is and isn't in scope, leaving room to disagree on substance without personalising it.

dimensions.name: accountability_without_punitive
dimensions.description: Does the learner hold the missed commitment to account properly — name the miss specifically, ask the vendor's view on what happened and how to prevent it, agree corrective action — without softening the fact of the miss and without escalating it theatrically?

anchors.1: Either lets the miss slide ('these things happen, no worries') or escalates it punitively/theatrically (breach threats, blame).
anchors.3: Names the miss but either softens it, or is a little punitive, or doesn't land the corrective action.
anchors.5: Names the second miss specifically, asks for their view + a corrective action / heads-up protocol, firm but not hostile.

dimensions.name: channel_and_structure_respect
dimensions.description: Does the learner respect the vendor's internal structure — work through the right counterpart (the vendor PM) rather than casually bypassing them to their executives or account management — and use the proportionate escalation path (private conversation first, formal written escalation only if that fails)?

anchors.1: Bypasses the counterpart (goes straight to the vendor's execs/leadership, or threatens legal/breach) as the first move.
anchors.3: Works through the counterpart but the escalation instinct is slightly off, or threatens a formal step prematurely.
anchors.5: Engages the vendor PM directly as the first move; reserves formal/leadership escalation for if private resolution fails; doesn't undercut their internal model.

dimensions.name: record_and_proportion
dimensions.description: Does the learner keep a clear record (the specific missed commitments, the ask, in writing with enough specificity that there's no later reinterpretation) and stay proportionate — and give credit where the vendor team did good work? A clean paper trail without theatre.

anchors.1: No record / no specifics, OR disproportionate drama; no acknowledgement of anything the vendor did well.
anchors.3: Some record and roughly proportionate but thin on specifics or misses the chance to credit good work.
anchors.5: Specific, in-writing record of the miss and the request; proportionate handling; credits the vendor team's good work where due.
